step.title: Step 13
step.decisionPreview: predict(expect_change=True, note='RIGHT moves player right; check if projectile follows')

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: SPACE.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 16, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. REQUIRED: call `predict(expect_change=... )` (or `predict(expected_grid=...)`) before each `action(...)`; then read `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats`. A `surprise` marks where your world model is wrong -- re-probe there instead of pressing on.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Player blocked from moving UP by blue projectile at rows 33-34. Player at rows 37-39, target B frame at rows 28-31. Need to clear the projectile or find another approach.
- Plan: Try SPACE again to see if it shoots the blue projectile upward, clearing the path to target B.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 15 of about 71 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The board changed but the objects are in the same positions! Player still at rows 37-39, blue projectile still at rows 33-34, target B frame still at rows 28-31. So SPACE didn't shoot the projectile or destroy the frame.
But the board did change. What changed? Let me check the pink bar at the bottom or some other HUD element.
I'm stuck. The blue projectile is blocking upward movement, and SPACE doesn't seem to do anything useful here. Let me think about alternative strategies:
1. Maybe I need to move RIGHT to avoid the projectile, then UP, then LEFT back to align with target B
2. Maybe I need to destroy the projectile first somehow
3. Maybe I need to approach from a different angle
Let me try moving RIGHT to see if I can go around the projectile. The projectile is at cols 17-18, and the player is at cols 16-19. If I move RIGHT, the player would go to cols 20-23, avoiding the projectile. Then I could move UP and then LEFT back to align with target B.
But wait, target B is at cols 16-19, and I need to be directly below it to shoot. If I move RIGHT and then UP, I'd be at cols 20-23, which is not aligned with target B.
Let me try a different approach: move RIGHT, UP, LEFT to get above the projectile, then DOWN to get below target B.
Actually, let me think about this more carefully. The player is 4x3 pixels (cols 16-19, rows 37-39). The projectile is 2x2 pixels at cols 17-18, rows 33-34. The target frame is 4x4 pixels at cols 16-19, rows 28-31.
If I move RIGHT, player goes to cols 20-23. Then I can move UP freely (no projectile blocking). Then I can move LEFT to get back to cols 16-19, but I'd be above the projectile. Then I could move DOWN to get below the projectile but... that would put me back where I started.
Hmm, what if I move RIGHT, UP multiple times to get above the target B frame, then LEFT to align with target B, then DOWN to get below it? That would work but would take many steps.
Let me try: RIGHT, UP, UP, LEFT, DOWN, DOWN to get below target B from the other side.
Wait, that doesn't make sense. Let me think again.
Current: player at rows 37-39, cols 16-19
Target B: rows 28-31, cols 16-19
Projectile: rows 33-34, cols 17-18
If I go RIGHT to cols 20-23, then UP to rows 33-35, then LEFT back to cols 16-19... but the projectile is at rows 33-34, cols 17-18, so I'd still be blocked.
What if I go RIGHT, UP, UP to get to rows 29-31, then LEFT to cols 16-19? But target B's frame is at rows 28-31, so I'd be inside the frame.
Actually, let me try a completely different approach. What if I move DOWN first to get away from the projectile, then RIGHT to go around, then UP along the right side, then LEFT to get above the projectile, then DOWN to get below target B?
This is getting complicated. Let me just try moving RIGHT and UP to see if I can get above the projectile.
Actually, wait. Let me reconsider the problem. The blue projectile follows the player. When I move left/right, it moves with me. When I move up/down, it stays at a fixed offset above the player. So if I move RIGHT, the projectile would also move RIGHT to stay aligned with the player. That means I can't escape the projectile by moving horizontally.
So the only way to get past the projectile is to... what? Maybe I need to destroy it somehow?
Let me try SPACE one more time, but this time let me check if the projectile moves or if something else changes.
Actually, I just realized something. The board changed when I pressed SPACE, but the objects stayed in the same place. What if the change was in the pink bar at the bottom? Let me check the pink bar.
Let me look at the dark gray object at rows 63, cols 60-63. That might be part of the HUD.
Actually, I think I need to reconsider the entire game mechanic. Maybe SPACE doesn't shoot upward - maybe it does something else. Let me look at the first SPACE action more carefully.
First SPACE: Player at rows 41-43, cols 32-35. Target C frame at rows 36-39, cols 32-35. After SPACE, the frame turned white.
But wait, was there a white block between the player and the frame? Let me check the initial state...
Actually, I don't have the initial state anymore. Let me just try a different approach: move DOWN, then RIGHT, then UP to see if I can navigate around the projectile.
Wait, I just realized: maybe the blue projectile is NOT following the player. Maybe it's a separate entity that happens to be in the same column. Let me test by moving RIGHT and seeing if the projectile moves with me.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: SPACE didn't shoot the projectile or destroy the frame. Board changed but objects stayed in same positions. Blue projectile at rows 33-34 blocks upward movement. Need to find a way around.
Plan: Test if blue projectile follows player by moving RIGHT. If it stays, I can navigate around it.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Move RIGHT to test if projectile follows
predict(expect_change=True, note='RIGHT moves player right; check if projectile follows')
action(['RIGHT'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
for n in nodes:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"Player: rows [{min(rows)}-{max(rows)}], cols [{min(cols)}-{max(cols)}]")
    if n['color'] == 'b' and n['pixels'] == 4:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"Blue: rows [{min(rows)}-{max(rows)}], cols [{min(cols)}-{max(cols)}]")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Blue: rows [25-26], cols [45-46]
Blue: rows [29-30], cols [17-18]
Blue: rows [33-34], cols [21-22]
Player: rows [37-39], cols [20-23]
